Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Understand User Input: Database Context PostgreSQL with three tables claims adjusters claim_events. Schema Details Provided for each table. Ideal Process Flow R A E P N C Given POWL Model Contains anomalies Loop between E and P XOR between N and skip Partial Order R A loop xor A C Task Identify anomalies Generate hypotheses Propose database queries

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Deconstruct Task 1 Identify Anomalies Loop E P allows repeated cycles deviates from ideal single pass E P XOR N skip allows skipping Notify Customer Partial Order Premature Close edge A C means C can occur right after A bypassing loop xor Concurrency Partial Ordering Issues strict partial order doesnt enforce xor C

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Deconstruct Task 2 Generate Hypotheses Why the E P Loop Business rule Claims might need re evaluation if approval fails Technical Workflow system allows re triggering Data Modeling Process mining discovered loops Why the XOR N skip Business rule Some claims might not require notification Technical Notification step optional Miscommunication Customer service handles separately Why Premature Close A C Business rule Fast track closure Technical Adjusters can manually close Process Drift Workaround Modeling Error Partial order incorrectly specified

Name: Abductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Deconstruct Task 3 Propose Database Verification Strategies Need to write SQL queries against claim_events to detect a Claims closed without proper E or P b Claims with multiple E P cycles c Claims where N is skipped d Claims where C happens before E P N Key SQL Concepts Window functions conditional aggregation Query 1 Premature Closure Find claims where C timestamp earlier than E or P Query 2 Multiple E P Cycles Count occurrences of E and P per claim Query 3 Skipped Notification Claims that reached C but never had N Query 4 Sequence Violations Check timestamp ordering R A E P N C

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Draft Query 1 Premature Closure WITH event_times AS SELECT claim_id MIN timestamp FILTER WHERE activity A AS t_A MIN timestamp FILTER WHERE activity E AS t_E etc FROM claim_events GROUP BY claim_id SELECT claim_id FROM event_times WHERE t_C IS NOT NULL AND t_E IS NULL OR t_P IS NULL OR t_C t_E OR t_C t_P Draft Query 2 Loop Detection SELECT claim_id COUNT FILTER WHERE activity E AS eval_count COUNT FILTER WHERE activity P AS approve_count GROUP BY claim_id HAVING COUNT FILTER WHERE activity E 1 OR COUNT FILTER WHERE activity P 1 Draft Query 3 Skipped Notification SELECT claim_id GROUP BY claim_id HAVING COUNT FILTER WHERE activity C 0 AND COUNT FILTER WHERE activity N 0 Draft Query 4 Sequence Violations WITH first_occurrence AS SELECT claim_id MIN timestamp FILTER WHERE activity R AS t_R etc SELECT claim_id FROM first_occurrence WHERE t_A t_R OR t_E t_A etc

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: Structure the Response Organize into three clear sections Section 1 Anomaly Identification list and explain Section 2 Hypotheses Generation provide reasons Section 3 Database Verification Strategies provide concrete PostgreSQL queries Self Correction Refinement POWL model uses StrictPartialOrder edge A C means C after A but no xor C SQL FILTER clause fine Add query joining with claims for claim_type correlation Note handling concurrent timestamps

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Self Correction Verification during thought Check POWL loop semantics LOOP with children E P executes E then optionally P and loop back SQL handles NULLs using MIN for first occurrence Add note about indexing for performance Explicitly map each query to hypothesis All good Proceed
